parentQuestion: Trauma to a nerve of the brachial plexus may cause wrist drop.

options: The wrist hanging limply because extensor muscles are paralyzed
scaffolds.explain: WRIST DROP is when the wrist HANGS LIMPLY because the EXTENSOR muscles are paralyzed. Without these muscles, gravity pulls the wrist down into flexion.

wrongExplains.0: Wrist drop is a medical sign of nerve injury, not a dance.
wrongExplains.2: It's a pathological condition, not an accessory.
wrongExplains.3: It's caused by nerve damage, not exercise.

options: The nerve network (C5-T1) that supplies the entire arm
scaffolds.explain: The BRACHIAL PLEXUS is the NERVE NETWORK formed from C5-T1 that supplies the ENTIRE ARM — all movement and sensation in the shoulder, arm, forearm, and hand.

wrongExplains.0: The brachial plexus supplies the arm. Leg nerves come from lumbar/sacral plexus.
wrongExplains.2: The plexus is a nerve network. The clavicle and scapula are shoulder bones.
wrongExplains.3: It's nerves, not muscle. Muscles are supplied BY the plexus.

options: Bending the wrist down toward the palm
Lifting the hand upward (back of hand toward forearm)
scaffolds.explain: WRIST EXTENSION is LIFTING the hand UPWARD so the back of the hand moves toward the forearm. When you wave goodbye, you're extending your wrist. Without extension, the hand hangs down.

wrongExplains.0: Bending down is flexion. Extension lifts the hand upward.
wrongExplains.2: Rotation is different (pronation/supination). Extension lifts the hand.
wrongExplains.3: Finger spreading is abduction. Wrist extension lifts the whole hand.

scaffolds.q: Which nerve controls wrist and finger extension?
scaffolds.explain: The RADIAL NERVE controls ALL the EXTENSOR muscles on the back of the forearm. It's the nerve for wrist extension, finger extension, and thumb extension.

wrongExplains.0: The median nerve controls flexion and thumb opposition, not extension.
wrongExplains.2: The ulnar nerve controls hand intrinsics. Radial controls extensors.
wrongExplains.3: The musculocutaneous nerve controls elbow flexion (biceps), not wrist extension.

scaffolds.q: Does the radial nerve come from the brachial plexus?
options: No, it comes from the lumbar plexus
Yes, it arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus
No, it comes directly from the brain
No, it comes from the sacral plexus
scaffolds.explain: YES! The radial nerve arises from the POSTERIOR CORD of the BRACHIAL PLEXUS (C5-T1). It's one of the major terminal branches of this plexus.

wrongExplains.0: The lumbar plexus supplies the thigh. The radial nerve is from the brachial plexus.
wrongExplains.2: Peripheral nerves come from the spinal cord via plexuses, not directly from the brain.
wrongExplains.3: The sacral plexus supplies the leg. The radial nerve is for the arm.

scaffolds.explain: RADIAL NERVE INJURY causes wrist drop. Without the radial nerve, the extensor muscles can't contract, and gravity pulls the wrist down into flexion.

wrongExplains.0: Median nerve injury causes 'hand of benediction' and thenar weakness, not wrist drop.
wrongExplains.2: Ulnar nerve injury causes 'claw hand,' not wrist drop.
wrongExplains.3: The sciatic nerve is in the leg. Wrist drop is from radial nerve damage in the arm.

scaffolds.q: Where can the radial nerve be injured?
options: In the spiral groove of the humerus (mid-arm fractures)
scaffolds.explain: The radial nerve is vulnerable in the SPIRAL GROOVE of the humerus. A MID-SHAFT HUMERAL FRACTURE can crush or cut the nerve against the bone.

wrongExplains.0: The nerve can be injured at multiple locations. The spiral groove is most common.
wrongExplains.2: Wrist injuries can cause different problems. The spiral groove is classic for wrist drop.
wrongExplains.3: Shoulder injuries can affect other nerves. The humerus is key for radial nerve.

scaffolds.q: If the radial nerve is a brachial plexus branch, is radial nerve injury technically brachial plexus injury?
options: Yes, injury to any branch is injury to that plexus's territory
scaffolds.explain: YES! The radial nerve IS a brachial plexus branch. Trauma to the radial nerve is technically trauma to a nerve OF the brachial plexus. The statement is TRUE.

wrongExplains.0: They're connected — the radial nerve arises FROM the brachial plexus.
wrongExplains.2: Injury to any branch affects brachial plexus function in that territory.
wrongExplains.3: The anatomical relationship is the same regardless of age.

scaffolds.q: Can trauma to a brachial plexus nerve cause wrist drop?
options: No, wrist drop is only from spinal cord injury
Yes, specifically if the radial nerve is damaged
No, wrist drop affects only the legs
Yes, but only from median nerve injury
scaffolds.explain: YES! The RADIAL NERVE is a brachial plexus nerve. Damage to it causes wrist drop. The statement is TRUE — brachial plexus trauma CAN cause wrist drop (specifically radial nerve injury).

wrongExplains.0: Wrist drop commonly occurs from peripheral radial nerve injury, not just spinal cord.
wrongExplains.2: The wrist is in the arm. Wrist drop is an arm problem from radial nerve damage.
wrongExplains.3: Median nerve injury causes different deficits. Radial nerve injury causes wrist drop.
